summary: Gruber defends Apple's decision to prohibit background apps on the iPhone, responding to Ian Betteridge's argument that OS makers shouldn't prevent poorly-written applications. He argues that the iPhone's restriction is a deliberate trade-off: users gain the guarantee that no third-party app can degrade system performance or battery life. He illustrates the problem with scenarios where typical users wouldn't know which background apps are causing battery drain or slowdowns. Gruber concludes that demanding the freedom to install potentially system-degrading software is reasonable, but the iPhone simply isn't designed for that philosophy.
key_points: A new platform can be innovative precisely by being different from established norms, including restricting background apps
The inability to screw up your system is itself a feature, not merely a limitation
Typical users lack the technical knowledge to diagnose which background apps cause battery drain or performance issues
Even well-behaved background apps can collectively degrade performance when running simultaneously
The right to shoot yourself in the foot is reasonable but belongs on platforms designed for it, like the Mac
money_quotes: What's wrong with "since now"? A new platform can't be innovative if it isn't different.
No matter what you install from the App Store, you *can't* screw up your system.
A likely reaction would simply be to regret ever having junked up their iPhone with any third-party apps at all.
What's the advice for the typical user supposed to be? "Have fun with the App Store, but don't install too much crap"?

If you truly demand the right to be able to shoot yourself in the foot with the software you install on your phone — which is a perfectly reasonable desire, and is how things work on the Mac — then the non-jailbroken iPhone isn't for you.
key_insight: Restricting background apps on the iPhone is not a limitation but a deliberate design trade-off that guarantees typical users can never degrade their own device by installing third-party software.
